behaviors.description: Review input is scoped to changed lines only, not the full file content
behaviors.justification: A unit test with a mocked diff object could pass even if the diff parser is broken. The failure boundary is in the diff parsing logic, which needs realistic diff format (with headers, context lines, additions, deletions) to expose edge cases. A mock with naive 'return all lines' would fail an integration test but pass a unit test with oversimplified mocks.
behaviors.test_description: Create a file diff with 50 total lines (10 unchanged, 20 changed, 20 outside scope). Run the review scoping logic and verify the input to the analysis step contains exactly the 20 changed lines, not all 50. This would catch off-by-one errors, missing context line handling, or incorrect hunk boundary detection.

behaviors.description: Inline comments are attached to the correct positions in the diff view
behaviors.justification: A mocked GitHub API would always accept any line number. Correctness here depends on GitHub's diff view semantics (diff line numbers ≠ file line numbers). Only real GitHub API or a test instance that enforces those semantics would catch off-by-one errors, incorrect hunk offset calculations, or confusion between 'position in diff' vs 'position in file'. Mocking would hide these failures entirely.
behaviors.test_description: Post a comment to a real test PR (or GitHub Actions test instance) with the calculated diff position. Retrieve the PR and verify the comment appears at the visually correct line in the diff view. This catches failures where line number calculations are wrong—a common bug when converting between diff positions and file line numbers.

behaviors.description: Agent identifies at least one real defect in a PR containing a known bug
behaviors.justification: The core claim is about LLM capability—what the model can actually detect. A mocked agent would always return the bugs you hardcoded. A System test with real diff posting but mocked analysis would miss the failure entirely. Only running real LLM inference on real code can verify the agent's analysis capability.
behaviors.test_description: Create a PR containing a known introduced bug (e.g., missing null check, unhandled exception path, type mismatch). Run the review agent with real LLM inference and verify at least one finding references the bug. This directly tests the model's code review capability—if the model can't detect the bug, the test fails.

behaviors.description: Every finding in summary has a matching inline comment; no summary-only findings
behaviors.justification: This tests the code logic that maps findings → comments → summary. You can mock LLM findings, but you need real code execution to catch bugs in the mapping (e.g., skipping certain findings, deduplication errors, off-by-one in the loop). A System test (real GitHub) adds safety but isn't required—the bug is in the code, not the API. However, race conditions in real posting could cause mismatches, making System safer for production confidence.
behaviors.test_description: Run the full review pipeline with mocked LLM output (5 specific findings). Verify that exactly 5 inline comments are posted and the summary contains exactly those 5 findings with no extras or omissions. Then verify each summary finding's location/description matches an inline comment. This catches bugs in the code that filters or transforms findings.

behaviors.description: Full sequence (read diff, analyze, post inline comments, post summary) completes without manual intervention on a real PR
behaviors.justification: This requires real PR reading (System), real LLM inference (Agentic), real GitHub posting (System), and sequencing them all together (Workflow). Each step has real failure modes: GitHub API timeouts, LLM response parsing, comment posting failures, summary format issues. Only end-to-end execution with all real components can catch integration failures between steps.
behaviors.test_description: Create a test PR with a small code change and a deliberate issue. Execute the full agent pipeline against the real test PR. Verify: (1) diff is fetched correctly, (2) at least one finding is generated, (3) inline comments appear on the PR, (4) summary comment is posted, (5) no errors or manual intervention was required. This is a smoke test that the entire workflow is wired correctly.
